funFact: The world's largest fish — up to 40 feet long — the whale shark feeds entirely on plankton and small fish, filtering thousands of gallons of water each hour.
description: The whale shark is a slow-moving filter feeder and the largest known fish species on Earth. Each individual has a unique pattern of spots and stripes — like a fingerprint — which researchers use to identify and track them across ocean basins. They are known to peacefully interact with divers.
habitat: Tropical and warm temperate seas worldwide; open ocean and productive coastal areas, especially near upwelling zones
geographicRangeDescription: Found in all tropical and warm temperate ocean basins between roughly 30°N and 35°S. Seasonal aggregations draw large numbers to specific feeding hotspots, including Ningaloo Reef in Western Australia, the Bay of La Paz in Mexico, Djibouti's Gulf of Tadjoura, and Belize's Gladden Spit. Satellite tagging has revealed transoceanic migrations spanning thousands of miles.
diet: Plankton, fish eggs, krill, small fish, and squid
size: Up to 12 m (40 ft); possibly larger

vitalSigns.label: Max Length
vitalSigns.value: ~12 m (40 ft) typical; up to ~18 m (59 ft) reported

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: Ovoviviparous; up to ~300 embryos documented in a single female; biennial to triennial cycle

vitalSigns.label: Age at Maturity
vitalSigns.value: ~25–30 years — among the slowest of any shark

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Plankton, fish eggs, krill, small fish, and squid; filters thousands of gallons per hour

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Extremely low — late maturity makes population recovery decades-long

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Unique spot/stripe pattern used for individual ID; ram filtration through dermal denticle-lined gill rakers

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical and warm temperate seas worldwide; open ocean and productive coastal upwelling zones

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to ~1,928 m (6,325 ft); most feeding activity in upper 200 m

populationTrendMeta.note: Derived from the IUCN 2016 Endangered assessment, which estimated a ~63% global decline over 75 years. Absolute counts are highly uncertain; the current range of ~100,000–240,000 individuals reflects wide confidence intervals in sighting and photo-ID studies.

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Plankton productivity declining from ocean warming and acidification

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indian Ocean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Plankton zones shifting due to altered monsoon-driven upwelling

preyDeclineRegions.name: Arabian Sea
preyDeclineRegions.note: Upwelling zones showing reduced seasonal productivity

preyDeclineRegions.name: Coral Triangle
preyDeclineRegions.note: Reef productivity and plankton density under increasing pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: Gulf of Mexico
preyDeclineRegions.note: Seasonal plankton aggregations disrupted by warming surface waters

preyDeclineRegions.name: Caribbean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Key aggregation sites remain productive; some seasonal variability

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Remaining targeted fishing in unprotected waters; high purse seine bycatch

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indian Ocean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Purse seine and gillnet bycatch; some directed hunting in unprotected waters

fishingPressureRegions.name: Arabian Sea
fishingPressureRegions.note: Dense shipping lanes causing frequent strikes; artisanal net bycatch

fishingPressureRegions.name: Coral Triangle
fishingPressureRegions.note: Entanglement in artisanal nets; some direct take in limited fisheries

fishingPressureRegions.name: Gulf of Mexico
fishingPressureRegions.note: Bycatch in tuna seine fisheries; boat traffic at aggregation sites

fishingPressureRegions.name: Caribbean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Protected in most national waters; lower directed fishing pressure

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — open ocean range; shipping lanes and tourism pressure at aggregation sites

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — plankton productivity reduced by warming seas

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — bycatch and ship strikes; protected in many waters

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — CITES Appendix II; protected in many national EEZs

threats.name: Ship Strikes
threats.description: Slow-moving and surface-dwelling, whale sharks are frequently struck by vessels. Propeller wounds are documented globally, and strikes often go unreported in open-ocean shipping lanes.

threats.name: Fishing Gear Entanglement
threats.description: Large body size does not prevent entanglement in purse seines and gill nets. Feeding aggregations near productive coastal upwelling zones overlap heavily with active fisheries.

threats.name: Tourism Pressure
threats.description: Unregulated swim-with interactions cause chronic stress, alter feeding behavior, and expose sharks to repeated boat traffic at known aggregation sites.

threats.name: Plastic Ingestion
threats.description: Filter feeding ingests microplastics and larger debris alongside plankton. Plastic concentrations in surface waters are highest in the tropical feeding grounds this species depends on.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Shifting ocean temperatures and declining plankton productivity reduce food availability and force longer migrations between feeding aggregations.

actionItems.title: Choose Responsible Whale Shark Tourism
actionItems.description: If you visit a whale shark aggregation site, select operators that follow established codes of conduct: no touching, minimum approach distances, no flash photography. Irresponsible interactions cause chronic stress and disrupt feeding.

actionItems.title: Reduce Plastic and Ocean Pollution
actionItems.description: As filter feeders, whale sharks ingest microplastics alongside plankton. Reducing single-use plastic consumption, supporting coastal cleanups, and advocating for plastic regulation directly help this species.

actionItems.title: Contribute to Citizen Science Photo-ID
actionItems.description: Researchers use spot patterns to track individual whale sharks across ocean basins. Submit underwater photos taken during dives to the Wildbook for Whale Sharks citizen science database.

actionItems.title: Support Marine Protected Areas at Aggregation Sites
actionItems.description: Key feeding aggregations in the Coral Triangle, Gulf of Mexico, and Indian Ocean need robust marine protection. Contact representatives to support expanded MPAs at known whale shark hotspots.

actionItems.title: Support Whale Shark Conservation Organizations
actionItems.description: Donate to or follow the work of groups like the Marine Megafauna Foundation, Shark Research Institute, and Shark Trust that fund tagging, research, and advocacy for whale shark protections globally.
